solution:
Efficiency η=PoutputPinput=ESISEPIP

⇒0.8=240IS4000

⇒IS=3200240=13.33 A

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.

  List-I   List-II
(A) AC generator (I) Detects the presence of current in the circuit
(B) Galvanometer (II) Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy
(C) Transformer (III) Works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit
(D) Metal detector (IV) Changes an alternating voltage for smaller or greater value
question:
Match List - I with List - II.

  List-I   List-II
(A) AC generator (I) Detects the presence of current in the circuit
(B) Galvanometer (II) Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy
(C) Transformer (III) Works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit
(D) Metal detector (IV) Changes an alternating voltage for smaller or greater value
solution:
AC generator-- Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. A→II.

Galvanometer-- Detects the presence of current in the circuit B-I.

Transformer-- Changes an alternating voltage for smaller or greater value C-IV.

Metal detector-- Works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit D-III.

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, the magnitude of VL and VC are twice that of VR. Given that f=50 Hz, the inductance of the coil is 1 Kπ mH. The value of K is _____ .
question: In the given circuit, the magnitude of VL and VC are twice that of VR. Given that f=50 Hz, the inductance of the coil is 1 Kπ mH. The value of K is _____ .
solution:
Since the voltage across the inductor and capacitor is same, the circuit is in resonance. Therefore, the current in the circuit will be,

i=VR=2205=44 A.

The voltage across the inductor is double that across the resistor. Therefore, VL=2VR=440 V.

We know that VL=iXL=i×2πfL⇒L=44044×100π=110π H=100πmH

Therefore, K=1100=0.01≈0.

subject: Physics
q: In an LC oscillator, if values of inductance and capacitance become twice and eight times, respectively, then the resonant frequency of oscillator becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> times its initial resonant frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
question: In an LC oscillator, if values of inductance and capacitance become twice and eight times, respectively, then the resonant frequency of oscillator becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> times its initial resonant frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
solution:
Resonance frequency of an LC oscillations circuit is stated as 

ω0=1LC, here, L and C represents inductance and capacitance of circuit.

Now, when inductance and capacitance changes, i.e., L'=2L and C'=8C

Then, resonant frequency is ω=12L×8C=14LC

⇒ω=ω04

Thus, the value of x=14.

subject: Physics
q: Using a variable frequency a.c. voltage source the maximum current measured in the given LCR circuit is 50 mA for $V=5 \sin (100 t)$ The values of $L$ and $R$ are shown in the figure. The capacitance of the capacitor (C) used is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{F}$.
question: Using a variable frequency a.c. voltage source the maximum current measured in the given LCR circuit is 50 mA for $V=5 \sin (100 t)$ The values of $L$ and $R$ are shown in the figure. The capacitance of the capacitor (C) used is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{F}$.
solution:
From $V = 5\sin(100t)$, angular frequency $\omega = 100$ rad/s
At resonance in LCR circuit: $X_L = X_C$, so $\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$
Maximum current occurs at resonance: $I_{max} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R}$
$50 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{5/\sqrt{2}}{100}$ (verified at resonance)
From resonance condition: $100 \times 2 = \frac{1}{100 \times C}$
$200 = \frac{1}{100C}$
$C = \frac{1}{20000} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ F = 50 μF

solution:
The given function of current is, i=42sin2πTt+10 A

It means,  i2=42sin22πtT+100+2042sin2πtT

⇒i2=422+100

RMS value of the current is, irms=i2=121=11 A

question: When the rms voltages <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> are measured respectively across the inductor <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the capacitor <math> <mi>C</mi> </math> and the resistor <math> <mi>R</mi> </math> in a series <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></mrow> </math> circuit connected to an <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source, it is found that the ratio <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> <mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3.</mn></mrow> </math> If the rms voltage of the <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> is close to :
solution:
V L : V C : V R =1:2:3

V rms =100 V then V R =?

using  V= V R 2 + ( V c − V L ) 2

    x = 1 0 0 1 0 =10 10

subject: Physics
q: For a given series LCR circuit it is found that maximum current is drawn when value of variable capacitance is $25 \mathrm{nF}$. If resistance of $200 \Omega$ and $100 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor is being used in the given circuit. The frequency of ac source is ______ $\times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$. (given $\pi^2=10$ )
question: For a given series LCR circuit it is found that maximum current is drawn when value of variable capacitance is $25 \mathrm{nF}$. If resistance of $200 \Omega$ and $100 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor is being used in the given circuit. The frequency of ac source is ______ $\times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$. (given $\pi^2=10$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{C}}} \\ & \mathrm{f}_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{100 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 10^{-9}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{25 \times 10^{-10}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 5} \times 10^5 \times \sqrt{10} \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & =\frac{100}{10} \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & \mathrm{f}_0=10 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
As per the given graph, choose the correct representation for curve A and curve B
{Where XC= Reactance of pure capacitive circuit connected with A.C. source
XL= Reactance of pure inductive circuit connected with A.C. source
R= Impedance of pure resistive circuit connected with A.C. source
Z= Impedance of the LCR series circuit }
question:
As per the given graph, choose the correct representation for curve A and curve B
{Where XC= Reactance of pure capacitive circuit connected with A.C. source
XL= Reactance of pure inductive circuit connected with A.C. source
R= Impedance of pure resistive circuit connected with A.C. source
Z= Impedance of the LCR series circuit }
solution:
The formula to calculate the inductive reactance is

XL=ωL   ...1

The formula to calculate capacitive reactance is

XC=1ωC   ...2

As can be seen from equation (1), the inductive reactance varies directly with the frequency and equation (2) indicates that capacitive reactance varies inversely with the frequency.

Hence, curve A represents the capacitive reactance and curve B represents the inductive reactance.

q: A series LCR circuit is connected to an AC source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contains a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, and a capacitor of capacitive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>130</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power factor of circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
question: A series LCR circuit is connected to an AC source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contains a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, and a capacitor of capacitive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>130</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power factor of circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
solution:
Power factor in LCR circuit is given by, 

cos ϕ=RZ, where, Z is the total impedance of the circuit.

So, cosϕ=RR2+XC-XL2

Putting the values, we have

⇒cos ϕ=80(80)2+(60)2

⇒cos ϕ=80100⇒810

Thus, the value of x=8.

subject: Physics
q: The electric current in the circuit is given as $i=i_{\mathrm{o}}(t / T)$. The r.m.s current for the period $t=0$ to $t=T$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The electric current in the circuit is given as $i=i_{\mathrm{o}}(t / T)$. The r.m.s current for the period $t=0$ to $t=T$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
The RMS current is calculated using $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i^2 dt}$.
Given $i = i_0(t/T)$:
$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i_0^2\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 dt} = \sqrt{\frac{i_0^2}{T^3}\int_0^T t^2 dt} = \sqrt{\frac{i_0^2}{T^3} \cdot \frac{T^3}{3}}$
$= \sqrt{\frac{i_0^2}{3}} = \frac{i_0}{\sqrt{3}}$.

question:

take π2=10
solution:

At resonance,

XC=XL⇒1ωC=ωL⇒1ωC=250π⇒C=1100π×250π F=4 μF

subject: Physics
q: Consider the LR circuit shown in the figure. If the switch S is closed at t=0 then the amount of charge that passes through the battery between t=0 and t=LR is:
question: Consider the LR circuit shown in the figure. If the switch S is closed at t=0 then the amount of charge that passes through the battery between t=0 and t=LR is:
solution:
The current in the LR circuit at any time t is
I=ER1-e-RtL

Where E, R, L, I are the EMF, resistance, inductance and the current respectively.
∴ Charge flown through the circuit from time 0 to LR is 
(charge) ∆q=∫0LRI.dt=∫0LRER1-e-RtLdt
⇒∆q=ERt+LR e-RtL0LR
⇒∆q=ERLR+LRe-1-LR
⇒∆q=ER×LeR
⇒∆q=ELeR2=EL2.7R2

subject: Physics
q: For the LCR circuit, shown here, the current is observed to lead the applied voltage. An additional capacitor C′ , when joined with the capacitor C present in the circuit, makes the power factor of the circuit unity. The capacitor C′ , must have been connected in:
question: For the LCR circuit, shown here, the current is observed to lead the applied voltage. An additional capacitor C′ , when joined with the capacitor C present in the circuit, makes the power factor of the circuit unity. The capacitor C′ , must have been connected in:
options: Parallel with C and has a magnitude <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

Series with C and has a magnitude  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

Series with C and has a magnitude  <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math>

Parallel with C and has a magnitude  <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math>
solution:
Current is leading,  it means inductive reactance is more than capacitive reactance

For making power factor to be unity the value of C is to be increased.

⇒ another capacitor C′ should be connected in parallel. So in that condition XL=XC

⇒ωL=1ωCeq⇒Ceq=1ω2L

⇒C+C′=1ω2L⇒C′=1ω2L-C

⇒C′=1-ω2LCω2L

subject: Physics
q: Two identical capacitors A and B, charged to the same potential 5V are connected in two different circuits as shown below at time t=0. If the charge on capacitors A and B at time t=CR is QA and QB respectively, then (Here e is the base of natural logarithm)
question: Two identical capacitors A and B, charged to the same potential 5V are connected in two different circuits as shown below at time t=0. If the charge on capacitors A and B at time t=CR is QA and QB respectively, then (Here e is the base of natural logarithm)
solution:
Charge on capacitor after charging with a battery of potential V, Q=CV

Now, capacitor A is connected as shown in the figure.

As diode is in reverse bias, so capacitor charge remains same after time t=CR.

∴ QA=CV

In case B, capacitor is connected as shown in the figure.

Here, diode is in forward bias, so capacitor discharges and its charge decays as 

QB=Q·e-tRC

where, t=RC

subject: Physics
q: An inductor stores 16 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates 32 W of thermal energy due to its resistance when an a.c. current of 2 A (rms) and frequency 50 Hz flows through it. The ratio of inductive reactance to its resistance is $\_\_\_\_$. $(\pi=3.14)$
question: An inductor stores 16 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates 32 W of thermal energy due to its resistance when an a.c. current of 2 A (rms) and frequency 50 Hz flows through it. The ratio of inductive reactance to its resistance is $\_\_\_\_$. $(\pi=3.14)$
solution:
Given: Magnetic field energy $= 16$ J, Thermal power dissipated $= 32$ W, $I_{rms} = 2$ A, $f = 50$ Hz.
From energy stored in inductor:
$\frac{1}{2}L I_{rms}^2 = 16$
$\frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot 4 = 16$
$L = 8 \text{ H}$
From thermal power dissipated:
$I_{rms}^2 R = 32$
$4R = 32$
$R = 8 \; \Omega$
Inductive reactance:
$X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 8 = 2512 \; \Omega$
$\frac{X_L}{R} = \frac{2512}{8} = 314$

question: In the circuit shown in the figure, the ratio of the quality factor and the band width is _____ s.

For an LCR circuit,

Bandwidth =RL

=103 Hz

=310×27×10-6=1003

=10.

subject: Physics
q: The amplitude of a damped oscillator decreases to 0.9 times its original magnitude in 5s. In another 10s it will decrease to <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> times its original magnitude, where <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> equals :
question: The amplitude of a damped oscillator decreases to 0.9 times its original magnitude in 5s. In another 10s it will decrease to <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> times its original magnitude, where <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math> equals :
solution:
A=A0 e-bt2m

After 5 second

0.9 A 0 = A 0  e - b 5 2 m    ...... (i)

After 10 more second

A = A 0  e - b 1 5 2 m      ...... (ii)

From (i) and (ii)

A = 0.729 A 0

subject: Physics
q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, power factor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></math> is added to the circuit the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be :
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, power factor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></math> is added to the circuit the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be :
solution:
In case of LR circuit:

Given: XL=R

P1=cosϕ=RZ=RR2+XL2=12

In case of LCR circuit:

P2=cosϕ=RZ=RR2+XL-XC2=1

Therefore,

P1P2=1:2
